name: Jiangshi
body: It announces itself before it arrives: the dry creak of joints that will not bend, the rasp of grave-soil on silk, and the faint sweetness of a body kept too long, dressed for an office it can no longer perform. Stiff with rigor and robed as a Qing official, complete with the round winged cap and the long dark mandarin sleeves, the Jiangshi cannot flex knee or elbow, so it does not walk but lurches, hopping forward in short rigid bounds with both arms thrust out before it like a sleeper feeling for a wall. Its face is the colour of mould or old wax, the lips drawn back, and what it hunts is not flesh but breath, the warm qi that streams from the mouth of anything living. It is a corpse that failed to find rest: buried without the proper rites, dead too far from its ancestral village to be carried home, or stirred from stillness by some foul energy that crept in where the funeral prayers were missing.

It is blind, and it knows you only by the breath you cannot help but take. The old advice is the strangest and the simplest: stand absolutely still and hold your breath until it has hopped past, because movement and the small fog of your exhalation are the only things it can read, so that to be caught is, in a sense, to have given yourself away. There are surer remedies than waiting. The folk wards are many and oddly domestic: a mirror, which is said to terrify the creature with its own ruined reflection; sticky glutinous rice scattered on the threshold or packed into a wound to draw out the corruption; a switch of peach wood, long held to drive off evil; and a high board laid across the doorway, raised exactly so that stiff and unbending legs can never clear it. Best of all is the Taoist priest, who seals the thing with a strip of yellow paper inked in cinnabar and pressed flat to its brow, and at that touch it freezes mid-hop, harmless as a propped plank until the talisman is peeled away.

What lingers is the held breath, the inherited knowledge that survival once meant making yourself indistinguishable from a corpse, motionless and silent while a dead thing passes close enough to touch. The story most likely grows from a real and grim trade of old China, the corpse-driving of the Xiangxi mountains in Hunan, where two practitioners, a master and his student, were paid to carry migrant labourers home for ancestral burial, moving the bound bodies only by night and ringing a bell or gong so that villagers would bar their doors and not look out. Rigor kept those corpses rigid, and to a watcher in the dark they seemed to leap forward in unison. In the Singapore diaspora the figure has long since softened into something almost fond, the white-faced hopping vampire of the old Hong Kong films, yet ask an older relative and they will still tell you, with the faintest seriousness over the rim of a teacup, that the trick is to keep your mouth shut and not to breathe.
note: Folk wards against it are many and oddly domestic: mirrors, which are said to terrify it with its own reflection; sticky rice spread on the ground or packed into wounds; the wood of a peach tree, long held to repel evil; and a high threshold board across the doorway, raised so that stiff legs cannot clear it. The cinnabar talisman of the Taoist priest is the surest stop of all.

The legend most likely grows from a real and grim practice of old China, the long-distance transport of corpses home for burial, in which bodies were said to be moved upright over the roads at night so that those who died far from their ancestral villages could be returned to rest. Carried into Singapore and the wider Chinese diaspora, the Jiangshi found a second life in twentieth-century film and remains one of the most recognisable figures of Chinese supernatural folklore.

recountings.text: A commonly repeated origin story traces the jiangshi to a real Qing dynasty trade called corpse driving in the mountains of Xiangxi, Hunan. Migrant laborers who died far from home were carried back for ancestral burial by two Taoist practitioners, a master and his student, who moved the bound bodies only at night and rang bells or gongs to warn villagers to stay indoors. Because rigor mortis kept the corpses stiff, witnesses claimed the dead could only hop forward, which later fed directly into the hopping vampire image of Hong Kong cinema. This is widely-circulated cultural lore rather than one person's account.
recountings.attribution: The World of Chinese, 'The Real Walking Dead'

recountings.text: A frequently cited rationalist retelling explains the hopping motion as an optical illusion. Families too poor to pay for carts hired corpse drivers who lashed rows of the dead upright against long bamboo poles, then carried them like shirts on a rack; as the carriers walked, the bamboo flexed and bounced, so from a distance the corpses appeared to leap in unison. A priest leading the procession rang a bell because seeing a jiangshi was thought to bring bad luck. This is widely-circulated explanatory lore, not a personal sighting.

Classical Chinese sources frame the jiangshi through soul belief: a person holds three hun (ethereal souls) and seven po (corporeal souls), and when the good hun departs at death but the evil po lingers and seizes the body, a corpse reanimates. The Qing scholar Ji Xiaolan catalogued causes of such reanimation, including absorbed yang energy, a lightning strike, or a pregnant cat leaping over an unburied coffin. This is widely-circulated traditional lore drawn from classical texts, not an individual's report.
recountings.attribution: Wikipedia, 'Jiangshi' (citing Ji Xiaolan and Yuan Mei)

references.title: The Real Walking Dead (corpse-walking in Xiangxi)
Chinese Hopping Vampires: The Qing Dynasty roots behind the Jiangshi legend
Walking dead: old China practice of corpse herding
